Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only top-level variable assignments and source definitions. No command substitutions, backticks, or function calls (like `pkgver()`) exist that would execute code during sourcing with `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. All sources are fetched from official JetBrains domains (download.jetbrains.com, cache-redirector.jetbrains.com, github.com/JetBrains). Checksums are provided for all entries, confirming integrity. There is no obfuscated code, unexpected network requests, or any executable payload in the top-level scope. The `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` functions are not executed during the requested commands, so their content is out of scope for this gate. Therefore, running the specified `makepkg` commands is safe.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code executes during source fetch or PKGBUILD parsing.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious code executes during source fetch or PKGBUILD parsing.[/]

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/intellij-idea-ultimate-edition.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-k74mzk0f...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-k74mzk0f
Analyzing intellij-idea-ultimate-edition AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-k74mzk0f) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines sources for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures, including official JetBrains download URLs and a GitHub raw content link for `fsnotifier`. The `source` array entries use HTTPS from trusted domains: `download.jetbrains.com`, `cache-redirector.jetbrains.com`, and `github.com/JetBrains`, all of which are consistent with the project's upstream sources. The use of variable substitutions like `$pkgver`, `jbr_ver`, etc., is resolved at build time but does not introduce unsafe command execution during sourcing.

There is no top-level command substitution, `eval`, or code execution outside of function definitions. The `pkgver()` function is absent, so version calculation does not occur. The variables are statically defined, including `pkgver=2026.1.3`, which may be forward-dated but is not suspicious in this context. The `sha256sums` are provided and not set to `SKIP` (except for VCS sources, which isn't the case here), indicating verifiable integrity for all downloaded files.

Thus, running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will safely fetch and verify sources without executing untrusted or obfuscated code. The operations performed during this phase pose no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
Safe sources and no unsafe top-level code execution.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe sources and no unsafe top-level code execution.[/]

data.reasoning: Both models performed only the initial PKGBUILD gate audit (which passed as SAFE for sourcing/parsing) and then skipped the full audit because `makepkg --nobuild` failed to download sources. No build()/package()/prepare() functions or .install scripts were actually reviewed. The package's substantive logic (extraction, installation, file placement, fsnotifier handling) was never examined. The PKGBUILD top-level assignments using trusted JetBrains/GitHub domains with provided checksums are reassuring but only cover the sourcing gate, not the full package behavior. Therefore the correct verdict is inconclusive — there is no basis to declare the package safe or unsafe.
data.disagreement_analysis: There is no real disagreement. Both models reached identical outcomes: PKGBUILD parsing is SAFE, but the full audit was skipped due to source download failure. The trigger note 'shallow (0 files reviewed)' simply reflects that the download failure prevented any file-level review.
coverage_issues: Zero files reviewed beyond the initial PKGBUILD gate — build(), package(), prepare() functions not analyzed
No .install script reviewed (IDE packages commonly ship .install hooks for desktop integration/post-install actions)
fsnotifier binary fetched from github.com/JetBrains was noted but never inspected for how it is installed/used
Source download failure (likely large JetBrains tarballs or expired/forward-dated URLs) blocked the entire substantive audit
false_negatives: The package() function behavior (where files get installed, permissions, symlinks) was never reviewed — potential risk unknown
Any .install scriptlet that runs as root during pacman install was not examined
re_audit_focus: build(), prepare(), and package() functions in PKGBUILD
how the fsnotifier binary and JetBrains tarball contents are placed/executed
ensure source download succeeds (may need network access, larger timeout, or mirror) so files can actually be reviewed
learnings: A 'SAFE' verdict on the initial PKGBUILD gate must not be conflated with package safety — the tool correctly marked this SKIPPED, which is the right behavior, but downstream consumers should treat gate-only passes as inconclusive.

Source download failures (common for large binary IDE packages like JetBrains products) systematically block audits; the tool should support reviewing PKGBUILD functions and .install scripts statically even when sources cannot be downloaded, since those files exist in the cloned repo regardless of source availability.

For binary-repackaging AUR packages, the most security-relevant files (PKGBUILD build/package functions, .install hooks) are present in the git clone and do NOT require source download — file selection should prioritize static review of these even on download failure.
Consider a fallback mode: if `makepkg --nobuild` fails, still perform static review of PKGBUILD functions and any .install/.sh files in the repo rather than skipping entirely.
